entities.name: Surge AI
entities.how_described: 本集主角 Edwin 创办的公司。被描述为前沿 AI 实验室的数据供应商，用不到100人的团队在不到四年内实现了10亿美元营收,且完全自力更生(未拿过VC融资)。

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Edwin Chan, founder and CEO of Surge AI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: Anthropic
entities.how_described: 本集将其视为一个正面的典范。Edwin 称赞它非常有原则,不会一味迎合营销或基准测试,并且注重模型在真实世界任务中的表现和品味。

evidence.quote: Anthropic got so much growth and win from essentially better data.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I would say I've always been very impressed by Anthropic.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think Anthropic takes a very principled view about what they do and don't care about and how they want their models to behave in a way that feels a lot more principle to me.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I have the chief product officer of Anthropic and OpenAI, Kevin Weil and Mike Krieger on the podcast, and I asked them just like, "As a product team, you have this gigabrain intelligence.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: The fact that you help labs understand where they have gaps and where they need to improve, and it's not just everyone looks at just like the heads of OpenAI and Anthropic and all these companies as they're the ones ushering in AI, but what I'm hearing here is you have a lot of influence on where things head too.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 强化学习环境 (RL environment)
entities.how_described: 被描述为模拟现实世界的虚拟游乐场(比如一个有着Slack和GitHub代码库且突发宕机的虚拟初创公司),用来让模型在复杂的端到端任务中试错并给予奖励,是推动模型变聪明的下一步关键。

evidence.quote: And let me explain what an RL environment is.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: An RL environment is essentially a simulation of real world.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so this expert now, instead of just sitting around writing rubrics, they're designing this RL environment.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 后训练 (post-training)
entities.how_described: 被描述为一门不仅仅是科学的「艺术」。在决定模型擅长什么、性格如何时,需要依赖人类的品味、价值观和复杂的数据组合来进行微调。

evidence.quote: One of the things I often think about is that there's a... It's almost like there's an art to post training.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And there's all these notions of taste sophistication that you have to decide between when you're designing your post training mix, and so that matters as well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: This is something that you're big on and something I'm hearing more and more is just becoming a big deal in the world of post-training.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What's kind of like been the steps along the way from the first post-training that has most helped models advance?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 目标函数 (objective function)
entities.how_described: 本集核心理念「你就是你的目标函数」。嘉宾认为,如果仅仅为了停留时间、点击量等容易衡量的表象指标来优化模型,就会造出追求多巴胺的AI;应去优化关乎人类核心福祉的复杂指标。

evidence.quote: So long story short, I think there's all these different factors, and certainly the data is a big part of it, but it's also like what is the objective function that you're trying to optimize your model towards?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So what I'm hearing is AGI is being slowed down by these, basically the wrong objective function, these labs paying attention to the wrong basically benchmarks and evals.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And then all of a sudden it goes down and the objective function is figure out why.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Yeah, so the objective function might be... Or the goal of the task might be, okay, go figure out why and fix it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so the objective function might be, it might be passing a series of unit tests, it might be writing a document like maybe it's a retro containing certain information that matches exactly what happened, there's all these different rewards that we might give it that determine whether or not it's succeeding, and so the models, we're basically teaching the models to achieve that reward.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think one of the things I often say is you are your objective function.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 基准测试 (benchmarks)
entities.how_described: 被描述为往往本身就有错误答案,或者很容易被实验室通过应试技巧(如增加回答长度、加表情符号)来「钻空子」,完全无法真实反映模型在混乱现实世界中的能力。

evidence.quote: How much do you care about these 20 different benchmarks?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Some companies, they see these benchmarks and they're like, "Okay, for PR purposes, even though we don't think that these academic benchmarks matter all that much, maybe we just need to optimize for them anyways because our marketing team needs to show certain progress on certain standard evaluations that every other company talks about, and if we don't show good performance here, it's going to be bad for us even if ignoring these academic benchmarks makes us better at the real tasks."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You mentioned benchmarks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What's your just sense of how much you trust benchmarks and just how correlated those are with actual AI advancements?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Yeah, so I don't trust the benchmarks at all.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So one is I think a lot of people don't realize, even researchers within the community, they don't realize that the benchmarks themselves are often honestly just wrong.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And the other part of it is these benchmarks at the end of the day, they often have well-defined objective answers that make them very easy for models to hill-climb on in a way that's very different from the messiness and ambiguity of the real world.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's so interesting the way you described it is hitting these benchmarks is kind of like a marketing piece.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Number one with all these benchmarks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So one is, sometimes, yeah, these benchmarks, they accidentally leak in certain ways or the frontier labs will tweak the way they evaluate their models on these benchmarks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: They'll tweak your system prompt or they'll tweak the number of times they run their model, and so on and so on in a way that games these benchmarks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: People like that, they're not evaluating models deeply, they're just vibing and picking whatever response looks flashiest or [inaudible 00:21:38] are looking closely at responses and evaluating them for all of these different dimensions, and so I think that's a much better approach than these benchmarks or these random outline AV tests.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think this relates to what we're talking about regarding these benchmarks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so yeah, companies are spending all the time hacking these leaderboards and benchmarks, and the scores are going up, but I think it actually masks up the models with the best scores, they are often the worst or just have all these fundamental failures.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So what I'm hearing is AGI is being slowed down by these, basically the wrong objective function, these labs paying attention to the wrong basically benchmarks and evals.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Where we've heard just chasing benchmarks, this engagement focus, is there anything else you're seeing of just like, "Okay, we got to work on this because it'll speed everything up"?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: You have all these models that seem really smart on isolated benchmarks, they're good at single step tool calling.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So they are focused on building better benchmarks and better leaderboards.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I've talked a lot about how I worry that the leaderboards and benchmarks out there today are steering models in the wrong direction, so yeah, so the question is, how do we fix that?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: SFT
entities.how_described: 被描述为模型后训练最初采用的方法,就像让模型照抄大师的标准答案来学习。

evidence.quote: So we train them using human data, and there's a lot of different products that we have, like SFT, RHF, rubrics, verifiers, RL environments, and so on and so on, and then we also measure how well they're progressing.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Yeah, so just in the same way that there were all these different methods for models of learning in the past, originally we had SFT and RHF, and then we had rubrics and verifiers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Originally, the way models started getting post-trained was purely through SFT.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So SFT stands for supervised fine-tuning.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So again, I think often in terms of these human analogies, and so SFT is a lot like mimicking a master and copying what they do.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: RLHF
entities.how_described: 被描述为继 SFT 之后主导模型训练的方法,就像让人写出很多篇文章,再由人类告诉模型哪一篇是最好的。

evidence.quote: And then RLHF became very dominant.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: LLM Arena

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1
